I usually buy things like this in bulk to get it at a better or lower price. That turned out not to be the case with this box of Little Debbie, cakes and treats. Even after reading the listing looking at the picture if really looked like there was a lot more in there.When in actuality there's only five little boxes. There are five different kinds of treats in here one box of each. Yes that's what I get for ordering when I'm already laid down for the night and  probably too tired to be shopping online. But really the main picture on the listing should never be that misleading. If it had been partially my fault for being half asleep when I ordered them they would have gotten a much lower star rating. But since I obviously didn't read it close enough. I just went with one off.Mostly due to almost all of the pictures showing multiple boxes of each kind. Basically portraying that you're going to get a much better deal than you actually end up getting.  Little tricks like that are really one of my biggest annoyances, especially showing photos with three times what you're actually getting when you order it. Kind of an underhanded practice in my book. If you're going to get one box feet it should show one box of each, especially in the opening photo on the listing.Most of these were at least somewhat fresh. None of them tasted like they were extremely old or dried out so that's a good thing. But as for me I won't be purchasing them again. As I said I bought buy in bulk, so I can get a better deal. Unfortunately this one just wasn't quite worth it.Read more

Amazon Grocery's affordable Jasmine Long Grain Rice in the 2 pound bag is flawless, but only if you adjust the amount of water you add to the rice.  Instead of 2 cups water for every 1 cup uncooked rice, switch to 1-1/2 cups water for every 1 cup, and suddenly it's the best Jasmine rice you've ever tasted.  Should it be the way I cook my white rice, I simply take a large microwave bowl and put in 6 cups uncooked rice with a few pinches of sea salt, then I add 9 cups water and stir, then I cook it for 35  minutes in my 700 watt microwave (check it at 30 minutes to see if it needs more or less) and it comes out of the microwave as flawless sticky Jasmine white rice, the kind you get in the restaurants.  Consequently if I use the original measurements of 2 cups of water for every 1 cup uncooked rice, it comes out too soggy and tastes overcooked.I like tossing the Jasmine with up to a cup of green snow peas to add more nutritional value into the rice which I use mostly for greaseless stir fry dishes all cooked in the microwave, for instance you can combine a couple bags of frozen broccoli, or the new Amazon Asian stir fry veggies that appear to be replacing the big hitter frozen assorted bell peppers and onions (both are extremely good), then simply mix in steamed or boiled Chicken breast or thighs (I use 1.5 ounces of sliced chicken meat per meal), then I combine it with one of Minors sauces (they come in nice gallon jugs and I purchase specifically General Tso's, and can also add anyone's Kung Pao to the General Tso sauce to turn it into extremely tasty Kung Pao sauce if you prefer purchasing a bulk amount, then I top it with sesame seeds and crushed red pepper and peanuts (if Kung Pao) with 2-4 of the Amazon frozen potstickers with it (1/3 rice vinegar and 2/3 soy sauce makes an excellent dipping sauce) for an high quality lunch or dinner.  It's a versatile meal in that you can use other sauces with the same format (Thai is excellent).For a changeup, try 1.5 ounces of thinly sliced Beef (Amazon has a nice stew meat) with broccoli florets with a couple scoops of the Jasmine rice, also VERY good and good for you, and I'd highly recommend Kona Coast Island Teriyaki, then top with sesame seeds.Read more

If you are managing your Glycemic Index (GI) or tracking your glucose spikes with a CGM, this brown rice is a fantastic pantry staple—specifically because of how well it handles "retrogradation."Many budget brown rices turn into a mushy porridge when cooked, but this variety maintains a distinct, firm structure. This is crucial for health-conscious cooks because grain integrity is directly linked to digestion speed. The firmer the grain, the slower the gastric emptying, and the lower the insulin spike.My "Longevity" Preparation Method:I use this rice specifically for the "cooling hack" to increase resistant starch. I cook a large batch with a teaspoon of coconut oil, let it cool in the fridge for 24 hours, and then reheat it throughout the week.Texture: Even after reheating (I use the steam function on my rice cooker), these grains don't disintegrate. They stay separate and chewy, which is exactly what you want for lower GI impact.Flavor: It has a mild, nutty profile that pairs well with healthy fats (like olive oil or avocado) without overpowering them.Value:Compared to premium health-store brands, the quality control here is surprisingly high. I found very few broken kernels or husks in the bag.The Verdict:If you are just boiling rice to eat immediately, this is tasty. But if you are using food science techniques (like cooling and reheating) to turn your carbs into functional prebiotic food, this rice is the perfect substrate. Highly recommended for meal preppers.Read more

This is brown rice, what is there to really say? It's easy to rinse before use. I've never really paid attention to grain length since I'm not trying to write my name on it at a mall kiosk. I have a rice cooker, so I don't pay attention to the cook time, but I think in general, brown rice might take a little longer than white rice. Once cooked, it has a nice, soft, light sticky texture with homey, aromatic flavor. It did take maybe 3 weeks to arrive though. It's a good value and I planned ahead.Read more

Excellent value. I can’t advise on the taste as I use it to make homemade dog food. Which is not as unhinged as it sounds. My vet is very happy with her diet ( lean meat, veg n fruits that are dog safe, brown rice and eggs). There are so many recalls on dog food for lead, plastic pieces, salmonella etc. Making it myself is healthier and also cheaper than quality dog food. I have a ziplock bag in the freezer I put veggie scraps in ( pepper tops, green bean ends, carrot tips and peelings, over ripe fruit etc). Anything with freezer burn, meat I defrosted to make for dinner and we ordered Chinese instead gets re frozen and labeled dog etc). So that’s all “free” and I supplement with family pack of chicken breasts on sale from $1.99-$2.99 lb all the time, $1 bag of frozen spinach, peas n carrots etc. There is very little dogs can’t eat. Ok… I’m off my soapbox lol. Sorry I’m passionate about it. It’s healthier for the dogs and better for the planet.Read more

The media could not be loaded.As at least one other reviewer noted, there are worms in this product.  I bought two, and both were science projects.  After a couple months of purchase, I noticed that there were a few little moth-like insects fly around my apartment.  I was wondering where they were coming from.  Turns out, a few larvae managed to escape from the open bag and had pupated somewhere here in the Enchanted Ghetto.  I went to make rice the other day and noticed "moving grains of rice"--same size, same color as the rice.  Amazon is very good about things like this.  Despite being 3 months outside the return window, I got a full refund--as anyone should considering the circumstances.  The worst thing of all is that I'm killing dozens of these little flying moths all over my apartment.  Many apparently escaped from their rice-bag prison, but having essentially come from an infinite food source, I imagine I'll be killing many more of these stupid things for weeks.  This is super annoying!Very Best Regards,Tom Scott 🗽Author of Stack the Legal Odds in Your Favor ● Speaker ● Top-Rated Anti-Corruption Blog Contributor ● World's Leading Expert on the Corrupt U.S. Legal SystemRead more

Amazons Long Grain Brown rice (LGBR) is a good deal in the 5 lb bags. I can't tell any difference in my local grocers brown rice and Amazons. Grains appear exactly the same, this (LGBR) offers a great option to white rice which is more refined and offers less nutrients, protein and fibers. The more refined white rice isn't better tasting or have a better texture nor is it better for you. White rice is brown rice prior to processing. I always order brown rice at restaurants and serve brown rice at the house, it's a no brainer. LGBR cooks the same as white rice, 1 part rice and two parts water...ez peezee.Read more

Excellent value. I can’t advise on the taste as I use it to make homemade dog food. Which is not as unhinged as it sounds. My vet is very happy with her diet ( lean meat, veg n fruits that are dog safe, brown rice and eggs). There are so many recalls on dog food for lead, plastic pieces, salmonella etc. Making it myself is healthier and also cheaper than quality dog food. I have a ziplock bag in the freezer I put veggie scraps in ( pepper tops, green bean ends, carrot tips and peelings, over ripe fruit etc). Anything with freezer burn, meat I defrosted to make for dinner and we ordered Chinese instead gets re frozen and labeled dog etc). So that’s all “free” and I supplement with family pack of chicken breasts on sale from $1.99-$2.99 lb all the time, $1 bag of frozen spinach, peas n carrots etc. There is very little dogs can’t eat. Ok… I’m off my soapbox lol. Sorry I’m passionate about it. It’s healthier for the dogs and better for the planet.Read more

The media could not be loaded.As at least one other reviewer noted, there are worms in this product.  I bought two, and both were science projects.  After a couple months of purchase, I noticed that there were a few little moth-like insects fly around my apartment.  I was wondering where they were coming from. I went to make rice the other day and noticed "moving grains of rice"--same size, same color as the rice.  Amazon is very good about things like this. Amazons Long Grain Brown rice (LGBR) is a good deal in the 5 lb bags. I can't tell any difference in my local grocers brown rice and Amazons. Grains appear exactly the same, this (LGBR) offers a great option to white rice which is more refined and offers less nutrients, protein and fibers. The more refined white rice isn't better tasting or have a better texture nor is it better for you. White rice is brown rice prior to processing. I always order brown rice at restaurants and serve brown rice at the house, it's a no brainer. LGBR cooks the same as white rice, 1 part rice and two parts water...ez peezee.Read more

If you are managing your Glycemic Index (GI) or tracking your glucose spikes with a CGM, this brown rice is a fantastic pantry staple—specifically because of how well it handles "retrogradation."Many budget brown rices turn into a mushy porridge when cooked, but this variety maintains a distinct, firm structure. This is crucial for health-conscious cooks because grain integrity is directly linked to digestion speed. The firmer the grain, the slower the gastric emptying, and the lower the insulin spike.My "Longevity" Preparation Method:I use this rice specifically for the "cooling hack" to increase resistant starch. I cook a large batch with a teaspoon of coconut oil, let it cool in the fridge for 24 hours, and then reheat it throughout the week.Texture: Even after reheating (I use the steam function on my rice cooker), these grains don't disintegrate. They stay separate and chewy, which is exactly what you want for lower GI impact.Flavor: It has a mild, nutty profile that pairs well with healthy fats (like olive oil or avocado) without overpowering them.Value:Compared to premium health-store brands, the quality control here is surprisingly high. I found very few broken kernels or husks in the bag.The Verdict:If you are just boiling rice to eat immediately, this is tasty. But if you are using food science techniques (like cooling and reheating) to turn your carbs into functional prebiotic food, this rice is the perfect substrate. Highly recommended for meal preppers.Read more

I've been eating and using unrefined coconut oil for years. There are a lot of good brands out now that weren't back in the day. I used to buy from an online company years ago and they were quite expensive. I always have great luck with Viva Naturals. This large container will last awhile and the price is very good compared to others. You get good quality coconut oil. The label doesn't have to say "extra virgin", but just "virgin" to be the same unrefined, cold-pressed oil. If it's real white, smells like fresh coconuts, you've got a quality oil. This coconut oil will last at least 2-3 years if I don't run out of it. I put a big scoop every day in my collagen shake for health reasons. I need to microwave it just a little bit to get it soft enough to scoop. Since it is saturated fat (the healthy kind) it will get very firm at temps below 72 degrees or thereabout. If it's particularly warm in your kitchen/house, it will liquify some depending on how warm the air is. You can't go wrong with unrefined coconut oil. So many uses. I dab a little on a skin sore or dry skin and it's good. I put it on my chapped lips for a moisturizer. You can oil-pull for your teeth also. This stuff is made by God I believe. It's antiviral, antifungal and antibacterial. This is a good, cost-effective brand I recommend. If consuming on a regular basis, you'll need to start with a small amount and build it up to avoid any digestive issues. The oil is potent. And and one more thing, it's great to cook and bake with. I've been eating and using unrefined coconut oil for years. There are a lot of good brands out now that weren't back in the day. I used to buy from an online company years ago and they were quite expensive. I always have great luck with Viva Naturals. The label doesn't have to say "extra virgin", but just "virgin" to be the same unrefined, cold-pressed oil. If it's real white, smells like fresh coconuts, you've got a quality oil. This coconut oil will last at least 2-3 years if I don't run out of it. I put a big scoop every day in my collagen shake for health reasons. I need to microwave it just a little bit to get it soft enough to scoop. Since it is saturated fat (the healthy kind) it will get very firm at temps below 72 degrees or thereabout. If it's particularly warm in your kitchen/house, it will liquify some depending on how warm the air is. You can't go wrong with unrefined coconut oil. So many uses. I dab a little on a skin sore or dry skin and it's good. I put it on my chapped lips for a moisturizer. You can oil-pull for your teeth also. This stuff is made by God I believe. It's antiviral, antifungal and antibacterial. This is a good, cost-effective brand I recommend. I love coffee, it is the one vice that I have that I refuse to give up. I usually make my own coffee at home, using a manual grinder and a pour-over coffee maker. I buy really good quality beans because I only drink black coffee, so there is no sweetener or creamer to disguise the coffee in any way. On a whim, I tried Stok Cold Brew Coffee Espresso Blend, since it was Un-Sweet Black. WOW! This is absolutely delicious! It is incredibly smooth, almost velvety, and not bitter or acidic at all. It gives you a really good kick of caffeine, though I'm sensitive to it so I feel it easily (my head is buzzing because I'm drinking it now 🫨😂), and it also has a nice aroma. But when I looked at the ingredients, I was surprised that it said coffee and "natural flavor." Why would you need to add any additional flavor to black coffee? And what exactly is the "natural flavor?" That is the only reason for the four-star rating, had the actual natural flavor been fully disclosed, such as some type of extract, it would have been five stars all the way. I have been a gym owner and trainer for over 30 years, and I've tried every diet under the sun. I now follow a whole food, plant-based diet maybe 90% of the time for my health because I feel so great on WFPB, in terms of energy, endurance, strength, and better mood and memory, and I can eat as much as I want and still stay super lean. I don't believe in being completely rigid or inflexible about anything, hence the 10% leeway. But, I do like to know exactly what I am putting into my body so I can make an informed decision. Many folks also have food allergies or sensitivities so every ingredient in a food or beverage, even flavorings, colorings, etc. should be fully disclosed. Though I don't do low-carb, keto, or count calories (that just didn't work for me and I felt awful), I was initially curious about the 4g of carbs and 20 calories per serving, until I remembered that this comes from soaking the beans. This is incredibly good coffee, I just wish they would disclose the source of the natural flavor.Read more

Quaker's Instant Oatmeal Fruit & Cream Variety Pack has been a fun and convenient addition to my mornings. It comes with four flavors: Strawberries & Cream, Blueberries & Cream, Peaches & Cream, and Bananas & Cream. Right off the bat, I’ll say the Strawberries & Cream packets dominate the box, which is fine if that’s your favorite, but it’s worth noting if you’re here for variety.The oatmeal itself is tasty—sweet, creamy, and comforting. It’s super quick to make, too. Just add hot water, give it a stir, and you’re good to go. If you’re rushing out the door or just want something easy, this is perfect. The flavors have their own charm: the Blueberries & Cream has a nice tangy sweetness, the Peaches & Cream is super nostalgic, and the Bananas & Cream is mild but comforting. Strawberries & Cream, though, seems to be the crowd-pleaser for a reason—it’s rich and flavorful without being overpowering (my kids like to mix a blueberry & strawberry).That said, there are a couple of things to keep in mind. These aren’t plain oats; they’re flavored, which means there’s sugar and artificial flavoring involved. It’s definitely on the sweeter side, so if you prefer something less sugary or more natural, you might find it too much—it’s worth considering if you’re watching your sugar intake.Overall, it’s a solid choice for those busy mornings when you want something warm and satisfying without a lot of effort. Just keep in mind that it leans more toward a treat than a super-health-conscious option. If you’re good with that, it’s a convenient and tasty breakfast option that’s hard to beat.Read more

I don’t usually write long reviews, but this product deserves it. That alone should tell you how much I enjoy it.- Taste & Quality: The flavor is crisp, refreshing, and naturally sweet without being overpowering. It tastes like real apples, not artificial syrup. Amazon notes that packaging may vary, but the product inside has always been reliable.- Value for Money: Compared to other grocery store brands, this apple juice is a fantastic deal. With four different flavors to choose from – Maple & Brown Sugar, Apples & Cinnamon, Cinnamon & Spice, and Original – there's something to satisfy every palate and mood. With individual packets that can be prepared in just minutes, it's the perfect solution for busy mornings when time is of the essence. Simply add hot water or milk, stir, and enjoy a piping hot bowl of oatmeal that's ready to fuel your day.Taste-wise, Quaker Instant Oatmeal delivers on all fronts. Each flavor is perfectly balanced, with just the right amount of sweetness and seasoning to tantalize the taste buds without overwhelming them. Whether you prefer the warm, comforting notes of maple and brown sugar or the crisp, tartness of apples and cinnamon, you're sure to find a flavor that delights your senses.Nutritionally speaking, Quaker Instant Oatmeal is a smart choice. Made with whole grain oats and packed with fiber, it provides a hearty and satisfying breakfast that keeps you feeling full and energized until lunchtime. Plus, it's a good source of essential vitamins and minerals, making it a nutritious way to start your day.In conclusion, the Quaker Instant Oatmeal 4 Flavor Variety Pack is a breakfast game-changer that delivers convenience, flavor, and nutrition in every bowl. Whether you're rushing out the door or savoring a leisurely morning, this assortment has something for everyone. Quaker's Instant Oatmeal Fruit & Cream Variety Pack has been a fun and convenient addition to my mornings. It comes with four flavors: Strawberries & Cream, Blueberries & Cream, Peaches & Cream, and Bananas & Cream. Right off the bat, I’ll say the Strawberries & Cream packets dominate the box, which is fine if that’s your favorite, but it’s worth noting if you’re here for variety.The oatmeal itself is tasty—sweet, creamy, and comforting. It’s super quick to make, too. Just add hot water, give it a stir, and you’re good to go. If you’re rushing out the door or just want something easy, this is perfect. The flavors have their own charm: the Blueberries & Cream has a nice tangy sweetness, the Peaches & Cream is super nostalgic, and the Bananas & Cream is mild but comforting. Strawberries & Cream, though, seems to be the crowd-pleaser for a reason—it’s rich and flavorful without being overpowering (my kids like to mix a blueberry & strawberry).That said, there are a couple of things to keep in mind. These aren’t plain oats; they’re flavored, which means there’s sugar and artificial flavoring involved. It’s definitely on the sweeter side, so if you prefer something less sugary or more natural, you might find it too much—it’s worth considering if you’re watching your sugar intake.Overall, it’s a solid choice for those busy mornings when you want something warm and satisfying without a lot of effort. Just keep in mind that it leans more toward a treat than a super-health-conscious option. If you’re good with that, it’s a convenient and tasty breakfast option that’s hard to beat.Read more
